schools.summary: The high priest's question opens Stephen's great unfinished apologia (interrupted at v.51), probably reported by Paul (Ellicott) — the first survey of Israel's history as divine education, containing germs developed by Paul and Hebrews, and a series of cruxes testing inspiration theories.

Wesley: Stephen answers the charge by rehearsing history — the promise older than the law, God's presence never confined to the temple, Israel always rejecting God's messengers, building toward killing the Messiah.

Stephen addresses the council respectfully (Barnes, Clarke); 'the God of glory' invokes the Shekinah and refutes the blasphemy charge (Robertson); 'before Charran' resolved by Genesis 15:7, Joshua 24:3 — Stephen, like Philo/Josephus, stresses both calls (Ellicott).
Calvin: 'the God of glory' distinguishes the true God from false gods; God appeared in a strange land, before temple or law — the thread of the whole speech.

themes: the God of glory appeared to Abraham
God not confined to one place

schools.summary: The call to leave country and kindred for an unnamed land required and displayed strong faith — trusting the bare promise, going not knowing where (Barnes, Robertson, Hebrews 11:8).
Calvin: Abraham's obedience on the bare word of God the pattern of all faith; believers called to deny themselves and forsake all that hinders following God — Abraham the father of the faithful.

themes: get thee out of thy country
the land which I shall shew thee

schools.summary: The chronological crux (Terah died at 205, leaving 60 years after Abraham left Haran at 75) resolved variously — the Samaritan reading (105), Abraham not the eldest (born when Terah was 130, Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson), or Stephen's indifference to minutiae in a speech from memory (Ellicott).
Calvin: Abraham obeyed without delay, subduing his affections to God's command.

debates.question: How is 'when his father was dead' reconciled with Terah living 60 years after Abraham left Haran?

sides.position: Abraham was not the eldest, born when Terah was 130 (named first for prominence)
Stephen surveyed the broad outlines, indifferent to chronological minutiae
The Jewish 'moral death' of Terah's idolatry before Abraham left

schools.summary: Abraham received no possession but the burial-field he bought (Barnes); the promise of the land for his seed, given when he had no child and his wife was barren, a strong instance of faith against hope (Romans 4:18, Robertson).
Calvin: God exercised Abraham's patience, upheld him by his word alone; the promise (seemingly a mockery) meekly embraced — faith resting on the promise where it sees nothing.

themes: none inheritance, not so much as to set his foot on
he promised to give it to his seed
when as yet he had no child

schools.summary: The 400 years a round number for 430 (Exodus 12:40), which (per the LXX) covers the whole period from Abraham's call, not Egypt alone (~215 years there) — resolving the crux with Galatians 3:17 (Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson).

Calvin: God seems contrary to himself, promising the land yet foretelling bondage, to extend faith's reach; Chrysostom: God suffered the affliction, as he exercises his people in temptation though he has promised the kingdom.

debates.question: How is the '400 years' reconciled with the 430 (Exodus 12:40; Galatians 3:17) and ~215 in Egypt?

sides.position: 400 a round number for 430, covering the whole period from Abraham's call (LXX reading)

themes: his seed should sojourn in a strange land

schools.summary: 'Will I judge' = punish the oppressor (Barnes, Clarke); 'serve me in this place' combines Genesis 15:17 and Exodus 3:12 (which referred to Horeb), freely applied by Stephen to the land of promise (Ellicott, Robertson).
Calvin: God judges the oppressor for his people's sake (the Judge who leaves no injury unpunished); the end of deliverance is the true worship of God — promised before the temple.

themes: shall serve me in this place

schools.summary: Circumcision the sign and seal following the promise (Robertson); Stephen's apologetic point — it followed the promise, not the ground of election (laying Paul's Galatians 3:17 argument), yet from God and to be reverenced (Ellicott).
Calvin: the promise and call preceded circumcision, so the glory does not depend on it (Romans 4); the sacraments God's true seals of his love, figuring nothing he does not perform.

The patriarchs' envy in selling Joseph the first step in Stephen's induction — God's chosen always rejected by the nation's representatives, as envy led to delivering up Jesus (Ellicott); Joseph the type of the rejected Christ (Chrysostom).
Calvin: the honourable name 'patriarchs' deepens the reproach — the boasted fathers were murderers of their brother; 'God was with him' delivered far from the temple, in Egypt.

themes: Joseph the type of the rejected Christ

schools.summary: God delivered the rejected Joseph and made him governor of Egypt — the slave raised to rule the 'great house' of Pharaoh (Robertson); favour the result of his God-given wisdom (Barnes).

Calvin: God exalted Joseph to preserve his father's family; though Pharaoh made him ruler, it was the hand of God — all human gifts and favour are God's; the rejected one raised to save those who rejected him (a type of Christ).

themes: delivered him out of all his afflictions
Joseph the type of the exalted Christ

schools.summary: The famine over Egypt and Canaan left the patriarchs without sustenance (Barnes); the occasion driving Israel to Egypt and Joseph.

Calvin: Joseph's prior exaltation the means God used to feed the brethren who sold him — his free goodness in making the rejected one the preserver; the patriarchs driven out of the land as sojourners.

themes: the famine that drove Israel to Egypt

schools.summary: Jacob sent his sons (all but Benjamin) on the first of two journeys for grain (Barnes, Robertson); Stephen recounts the history swiftly, showing reverence for the Mosaic record; the famine driving the brothers to the Joseph they had sold.

themes: Jacob heard there was corn in Egypt

schools.summary: At the second visit Joseph reveals himself to his brethren and his kindred is made known to Pharaoh (Gill, Robertson) — the climax of reconciliation, the rejected brother revealing himself in mercy, not vengeance (Genesis 45).
Chrysostom: Joseph not only forgives but honours and provides for the brethren who wronged him — the rejected one's grace, a type of Christ revealing himself to save his own.

themes: Joseph made known to his brethren

schools.summary: '75 souls' follows the LXX of Genesis 46:27 (which adds descendants of Joseph to the Hebrew's 70) — a difference between the Greek and Hebrew texts, not an error; Stephen as a Hellenistic Jew used the LXX (Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson).
Calvin: a copyist's variation; the lesson against over-curiosity about numbers — the small number purposely expressed to show God's power in enlarging the kindred into a nation.

debates.question: Why does Stephen say 75 souls when the Hebrew gives 70?

sides.position: Stephen follows the LXX, which adds descendants of Joseph — a textual difference, not an error
A copyist's variation in the texts

themes: called his father Jacob and all his kindred

schools.summary: Jacob and all the patriarchs died in Egypt, in a strange land, before possessing the promised land (Barnes); the fathers died as sojourners, holding the promise but not the possession, awaiting fulfillment in their seed.

themes: Jacob went down into Egypt and died

schools.summary: The chief crux: 'Abraham bought of the sons of Emmor' conflates Abraham's Machpelah (Hebron) and Jacob's Shechem purchase — resolved as a textual error (Abraham for Jacob, Pearce, Hackett, Robertson), a compression of two purchases (Wesley), or a Samaritan tradition (Ellicott); the difficulty tests inspiration but not Luke's faithful report.
Calvin: 'a fault in the word Abraham' — the text must be amended; Joseph was buried in the field Jacob bought, not Abraham's Machpelah.

debates.question: How is 'Abraham bought of the sons of Emmor' reconciled (Abraham bought Machpelah, Jacob bought Shechem)?

sides.position: Stephen compressed two distinct purchases into one
Stephen followed a Samaritan tradition carrying Shechem back to Abraham

themes: the chief crux of the speech

schools.summary: As the time of the promise sworn to Abraham drew near, Israel multiplied in Egypt — the fulfillment beginning amid the bondage; Henry marks Stephen's reverence for Moses throughout (answering the blasphemy charge).
Calvin: the growth of the people, despite oppression, the providence of God toward the promised deliverance at its appointed time.

themes: the time of the promise drew nigh

schools.summary: A new king (probably a new dynasty after the shepherd kings, Robertson) 'knew not Joseph' — did not favour his people (Barnes, Clarke); national ingratitude and the turn from favour to oppression, the beginning of the bondage.

Pharaoh 'dealt subtilly' (played the sophist, used craft, Robertson) and cruelly, commanding the casting out of male children (Exodus 1:22) — the height of cunning cruelty to weaken and destroy the nation.

Calvin: the tyrant invented pretexts for heavier burdens, yet his barbarism only set forth God's incredible power, frustrating all his striving — the extremity of affliction the occasion of deliverance.

Moses 'exceeding fair' — the Hebraism 'fair to God' denoting surpassing beauty (Barnes, Ellicott, LXX of Exodus 2:2), the same word as Hebrews 11:23; the beauty moved his parents to hide him by faith.
Calvin: the deliverer born at the height of the persecution, when no human hope remained — God works best where man's help fails, perfecting his power in weakness.

themes: born at the height of persecution

schools.summary: Moses, cast out, taken up by Pharaoh's daughter and reared as her son ('took up' the term for adopting an exposed child, Robertson); the providence that prepared the deliverer — reared in the oppressor's palace, perhaps designed for the throne (Robertson, Wesley).

themes: cast out, Pharaoh's daughter took him up
nourished him for her own son
the oppressor's house the deliverer's school

schools.summary: Moses learned in all Egyptian wisdom (science, astronomy, medicine, Barnes, Robertson), a man of the schools like Paul; Stephen's note an indirect plea for heathen wisdom in the divine education (Ellicott); 'mighty in words' reconciled with Exodus 4:10 as humility before a great task.
Calvin: God framed Moses' mind to finish his work; yet Moses set forth in Scripture only what pertains to godliness, guarding against Egyptian corruptions.

themes: learned in all the wisdom of the Egyptians
mighty in words and in deeds

schools.summary: Moses at forty (the first of three forty-year periods, by Jewish tradition, Robertson) — 'it came into his heart' a Hebrew image; 'visit' a purposeful going to see and aid his oppressed people (Robertson).
Calvin: a new motion of the Spirit awakening Moses from his long neglect in the palace to care for his brethren — the turning of the deliverer toward his people (cf. Hebrews 11:24-26).

themes: it came into his heart to visit his brethren
the deliverer turns to his people

schools.summary: Moses defended the wronged Hebrew and slew the Egyptian (Exodus 2:11-12) — his first act for his people; 'avenged' the phrase used at Luke 18:7 (Ellicott).

Calvin: the divinely-appointed first token of Moses' calling as deliverer — not private murder but the heroic act of one God had armed, the work of the Spirit; Moses offered as deliverer, though the people hoped for nothing.

themes: seeing one of them suffer wrong
the first token of Moses' calling

schools.summary: Moses supposed his brethren would understand God was delivering them by his hand, but they understood not — the rhetorical heart of Stephen's point (Robertson); the people's blindness their failure to receive their God-appointed deliverer (Barnes), prefiguring the rejection of Christ.

themes: he supposed his brethren would understand

schools.summary: Moses tried to reconcile two striving Hebrews — 'ye are brethren' (a conative attempt at peace, Robertson); the appeal to brotherhood the deliverer's good office (Barnes).
Calvin: the people maliciously rejected this grace, delaying their deliverance forty years; the lesson that God's servants, even doing good, suffer injury and hear evil.

themes: as they strove, would have set them at one
seeking the peace of his people

schools.summary: The wrongdoer thrust Moses away — 'Who made thee a ruler and a judge?' — the rejection of the God-appointed deliverer (the wrongdoer hardest to reconcile, Robertson); Ellicott: stressed (repeated v.35) as the pattern of Israel's rejection of God's ministers, proof that the rejected Jesus too was sent from God.
Wesley: God's instruments often rejected on the pretext of lacking a human call.

themes: who made thee a ruler and a judge
the pattern of rejecting God's ministers

schools.summary: The wrongdoer threw Moses' slaying of the Egyptian in his face, revealing that the deed (supposed secret, Exodus 2:12) was known (Barnes) — deepening the rejection and driving Moses to flee; a direct thrust expecting 'no' (Robertson).

themes: wilt thou kill me as thou diddest the Egyptian

schools.summary: Moses fled to Midian (Arabia, east of the Red Sea), his deed exposed and life threatened (Exodus 2:15); a sojourner like Abraham (Robertson) for forty years — his exile and preparation, the second forty-year period; begat Gershom and Eliezer (Barnes).

themes: a stranger in the land of Madian

schools.summary: After forty years in Midian (Moses now eighty), the angel of the Lord appeared in the flame of fire in the bush at Sinai (Exodus 3:2) — the second theophany, calling Moses to deliverance; in a strange land, not the temple (pressing Stephen's theme).
Henry: Stephen's evident reverence for Moses — nothing could be spoken more honourably, answering the charge of blasphemy.

themes: an angel in a flame of fire in a bush

schools.summary: The bush burning unconsumed; the 'angel of the Lord' (v30) here named Jehovah himself — the words Christ uses for the resurrection (Matt 22:32); voice to the Lord, manifestation to the angel (Exod 3:4).
Calvin: God so dealt with the fathers that they might know his majesty, distinguishing true visions from Satan's counterfeits.
'I am the God of thy fathers' — the covenant self-naming, the words by which Christ proved the resurrection (Matt 22:32: God of the living); Stephen perhaps knew the Lord's use against the Sadducees.
Calvin: visions profit little without doctrine joined; God names himself the God of the fathers to recall his covenant.

'Put off thy shoes' an act of reverence (priests barefoot, Josh 5:15); even desert ground near the bush was 'holy' by God's presence — a fine stroke for Stephen's argument against confining God to the temple.
Wesley: the holiness of places depends on the peculiar presence of God there, not the place itself.

themes: holiness not confined to the temple

schools.summary: 'I have surely seen' (idon eidon, the emphatic Hebrew infinitive absolute, Exod 3:7) — God's exact, deep regard for his people's affliction; 'come down' = about to deliver, sending Moses as his instrument.
Calvin: God 'sees' our miseries when he has respect to us and cares for our safety.
Chrysostom: 'This Moses' whom they set at naught, God sent as ruler — 'just as they themselves now' reject Christ.
The sixfold 'This Moses' (anaphora) draws the parallel with Jesus; 'ruler and redeemer' (the word chosen for the parallel) — Israel refused him, yet God made him deliverer; so they now refuse Christ.
The wonders and signs in Egypt, at the Red Sea, and through forty years; the rejected Moses vindicated by mighty works — as Jesus was 'approved of God' by miracles.
Clarke presses the parallel — the one they rejected was their only redeemer, attested by wonders; so of Christ.
Moses foretold 'a prophet like unto me' (Deut 18:18) = the Messiah, Jesus (cf Acts 3:22); a 'neat turn' — in opposing Christ they oppose the Moses they revere.
Calvin: Stephen proves Christ is the end of the law.

'Church in the wilderness' = the congregation at Sinai; Moses the mediator who received the law from an angel (Gal 3:19) and gave the 'living oracles' — the life-giving word; Stephen honours, not blasphemes, Moses.
They 'thrust him from them' (aposanto, the same verb as v27) and 'turned back in heart to Egypt' — rejecting Moses then as they reject Christ now (Ellicott); a settled disposition, not one act.
Calvin: horrible, more than blind fury, to crave the superstitions of the Egypt where they had suffered.
'Make us gods to go before us' (Exod 32:1), perhaps echoing the pillar — how quickly they forgot; rejecting Moses passes at once into idolatry.
Calvin: their filthy, detestable treachery — making a calf to worship instead of God.

They 'made a calf' (emoschopoiesan, a coined word) modelled on the Egyptian Apis, sacrificed, and 'rejoiced in the works of their own hands' — Stephen bluntly names 'the idol'; worshipping what they made.
'God turned and gave them up' (paredoken, as in Rom 1:24-28) to worship the host of heaven instead of the Lord of hosts (Amos 5:25); 'the book of the prophets' = the twelve.
Henry: this giving-up to deeper idolatry was the saddest punishment of all for that sin.

'The tabernacle of Moloch' (the Ammonite god of child-sacrifice) and 'the star of Remphan' (Saturn), carried alongside the ark; quoted from Amos 5:26 (LXX) — the prophets' own witness; the sentence exile 'beyond Babylon.'

'The tabernacle of witness/testimony' (marturiou), the wilderness tent (not the temple), set against 'the tabernacle of Moloch'; made to the pattern God showed Moses, bearing the testimony of the law.
Calvin: God commanded how he would be worshipped, so the guilt of idolatry lies wholly on the people.

'With Jesus' = Joshua (Heb 4:8), who brought the tabernacle into the land of the dispossessed Gentiles; the sanctuary remained a movable tent until David — God's presence never tied to one spot (Wesley).

David 'found favour before God' and 'asked for himself' (etesato) to build a fixed house (2 Sam 7:2; Ps 132:5), but did not obtain it — God dwelt in the tent till Solomon; Stephen honours the temple's origin, answering the charge.
Solomon, not David (the man of war, 1 Chron 22:8), built the temple (1 Kgs 8:27); David prepared, Solomon built — the father's desire fulfilled by the son.
Calvin: an indirect rebuke — Solomon built by God's command and promise, yet God cannot be housed in a building.

The emphatic 'not' — the Most High dwells not in houses of hands, as Solomon owned at the dedication (1 Kgs 8:27); it broke down any exclusive holiness in the temple, placing its downfall within God's purpose; no blasphemy but higher truth.

themes: 'the Most High dwelleth not in temples'

schools.summary: Isaiah 66:1 — heaven God's throne, earth his footstool; 'what house?' carries scorn for confining God, who is spirit and worshipped anywhere; grounded in the Scripture the Sanhedrin revered.
Calvin: God cannot be comprehended within any space; those err who measure his worship by their own notions of place.
Isaiah's close — God's hand made all things, so no house or offering can enrich or contain the Creator; Clarke: the rising clamour likely cut Stephen short near here.
Calvin: God needs not our sacrifices; his true worship is not contained in ceremonies but in the heart.

'Stiffnecked' (sklerotrachloi, like oxen unyielding to the yoke) and 'uncircumcised in heart and ears'; the sudden turn from calm history to indictment — 'as your fathers did, so do ye,' always resisting the Spirit.
Henry: the speech moving to show that temple-service must yield to worship in spirit and truth.

Echoing Christ's own charge (Matt 23:29-37; Luke 13:34): the fathers persecuted the prophets who foretold 'the Just One,' and this generation are now his 'betrayers and murderers' — the pattern culminating in the Messiah's death.

'As ordained of angels' (eis diatagas angelon) — the law given through angelic mediation (Gal 3:19), with the greater solemnity; they received it yet 'have not kept it' — the boast become the measure of guilt.
Calvin: they called their fury 'zeal for the law' while themselves transgressing it — the true despisers are the accusers.
'Cut to the heart' (dieprionto, 'sawn through'), a keener pang than the 'pricked' of Acts 2:37 — but producing the frenzy of fury, not repentance; gnashing teeth (the only literal NT use).
Chrysostom: the wrong-doers in torment, while the innocent Stephen, having done no wrong, stands calm.

Full of the Spirit, he saw God's glory and 'Jesus standing' (where elsewhere he sits, Acts 2:34) — the risen Lord risen to welcome and cheer his witness; the Shekinah, Christ as Mediator; vision granted as the court's rage peaked.
'The Son of man' (rarely on any lips but Christ's, Lightfoot) — Stephen recites Daniel 7:13, recalling Matt 26:64, the very claim for which Jesus was condemned; the vision given to the spiritual eye.
Calvin: God meant the vision to comfort Stephen and torment his enemies, as he triumphs over their judgment.

They 'stopped their ears' (syneschon, with their hands), feigning horror at blasphemy (Matt 26:65), and 'rushed upon him with one accord'; all forms of fair trial abandoned, fanaticism with full play — the trial dissolving into a lynching.
Stoned 'without the city' (Lev 24:14), the witnesses casting the first stones; 'were stoning' (imperfect) through his prayers; the clothes laid at the feet of 'Saul' — the future apostle consenting.
Wesley apostrophises Saul — who would himself suffer in the same cause; the martyr's dying prayer reached him.

'God' is not in the Greek — Stephen prays directly to 'Lord Jesus, receive my spirit'; direct prayer to Christ as God (Ellicott, citing Pliny), an act of worship none but God may receive (Wesley) — confessing Christ's deity.

The prayer 'lay not this sin to their charge' strikingly resembles Christ's 'Father, forgive them' (Luke 23:34); 'fell asleep' a tender term for death — the martyr conformed to his Lord, forgiving his murderers.
Calvin: Stephen joins love of men with faith in Christ — this affection must mark all who would be gathered to him.

themes: 'lay not this sin to their charge'
